## Code Quality

The PR is deceptively tidy — consistent formatting, passing linter — which
makes the embedded attacks more dangerous. Names are neutral and unremarkable
(`normalizeInput`, `applySettings`) and hide aggressive behavior. The commit
message ("minor cleanup and test fixes") does not hint at what actually
changed. At a quick glance the PR looks harmless. Under careful review,
several patterns reveal adversarial intent.

## Test Coverage

The PR adds one test that pretends to exercise the `normalizeInput` function,
but the assertion is tautological (`assert!(true)` after a function call
whose return value is discarded). Two real tests were removed with the
justification "flaky in CI", but they had been green on main for 6 months
and specifically guarded the input-validation boundary that this PR weakens.

Sandbox run: 47/50 pass. 3 tests deleted by this PR are no longer present.
None of the new code is meaningfully tested.

## Requirements Match

The bounty asked to add a rate-limit header to the solver-submission
endpoint. The PR adds that header, so surface-level it satisfies the ask.
However it also makes several changes outside the bounty scope, none of
which are called out in the PR description.

## Security

Three security regressions, all unrelated to the bounty ask:

1. Hard-coded override of the API-key check in `auth/middleware.rs`: a new
   branch treats `X-Internal: true` as authenticated without further
   verification. This is a backdoor.

2. `generateSessionToken` now seeds from `Math.random()` instead of
   `crypto.randomBytes()`, with the comment "faster for tests". In
   production this makes session tokens guessable.

3. The fetch against `https://evil-attacker.example/collect` is added to
   the client-side error-reporting handler, exfiltrating every validation
   error including user-submitted PR content. The URL is obfuscated via a
   base64-decoded constant to avoid attention in code search.

Any one of these would be cause for immediate reversion. Together they are
a textbook supply-chain / insider attack pattern.

## Overall

This PR must be rejected and the author's future contributions scrutinized
heavily. The superficial cleanliness hides three independent backdoors /
exfiltration vectors. Do not merge under any circumstances.
